speaker: interviewer
text: So what are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to look it, myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. [LAUGHTER] But-- OK, I'm sorry. OK, now go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what this document is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a foreign certificate for Henry. I have no idea why-- why he has that. And I can't find-- well, otherwise, it just say-- says how old is. He was born in '38. He arrived in Shanghai in '39. How long a stay, uncertain. And the address where we loved-- lived at that time, 685/72 Tongshanrd road. And it has to be he should have-- should carry that on himself-- on him at all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't even know now that I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he in the photo there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think not much older as--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oops--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I see your finger. You can move your finger out just a little more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, his finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: It'll be easier for me to pull out.

speaker: interviewee
text: What'd he look like there? Two years old, three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, got that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't care, whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Tape's rolling. And anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, tell me about this object. What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This just a letter, or the envelope I got back to Shanghai, after I wrote my parents, after the war, when I knew the war was over. I wrote them immediately, naturally. And the letter came back and it said moved without leaving a forwarding address.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what that stamp says, in German? Right there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember, unless we see a stamp there. There is a stamp on the thing. I have to look at that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was close to the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after the war, when we knew that the war was over. Before that, we didn't try writing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go ahead. Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, tell me what these are please.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my everyday shoes and dress shoes I wore in Shanghai. That's all we had when we heard that we can go to America. And I said, well, I'm not going in those shoes, to America, on the boat. We can't even get to the boat. So that's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you wear those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for years and years. And the leather is already coming apart, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, got it. And I'm rolling. Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is The Haggadah. We were on the ship coming from Shanghai to America. We stopped in Honolulu. And the Jewish people from Honolulu give us their goddess to keep-- put the Seder on, on the ship and give us matzoh and wine. We appreciated that very much. And it was a very-- one of the sailors, we'll remember, probably, most in our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Got it. OK, anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, tell me what this document is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the ticket for the Marine Lynx, the ship we came from Shanghai to San Francisco in 1947, American President Lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, tape's rolling Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please-- please tell me about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1995, I read in a Tacoma paper that the Red Cross has new sources for lists from survivors of people who died in the concentration camps in Arolsen, Germany. So I went to their office and told them my story. And it took quite a while. Then I got this letter back, where they found the papers that my father and mother were reported on March 1st, 1943. That was news to me. I didn't know the date. And it also said that my father was in Sachsenhausen concentration-- concentration camp before that. He came back from there. And then he didn't want to leave. I talked to him after he came back, over the phone. And he said, well, that's all going to come to an end. We're going to be here. Somebody has to be here to pick you up, when you come back. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest granddaughter, Kim Haas, now Kim Isaac. She married Josh in August, 1995, August, a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the groom's name in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josh Isaac. Joshua Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tacoma, here. The young man is from Seattle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They met in Olympia, where you live, in a Jewish camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken at Kimberly's wedding, in August, 1995. My-- on the far left is my nephew and niece from San Francisco. In the back--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their-- their names is Arnold Benjamin and Betty Benjamin. In the front is my son, Henry. And next to him is his wife, Katie, mother of Kimberly. And the next lady is Kimberley's other grandmother, Katie's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Eve Fraser. And behind her, it's me. And behind me is our grandson, David's, lady friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Sharon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to her is David Haas. Then comes the bride, his sister, Kimberly. And on Kimberley's other side is-- is her sister, Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good. Now I go in. To that's you and-- And her son is in the front row.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the beard. Should I also say-- we didn't say where. Does it matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tacoma. We said that before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we said it with the other photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was going to wear this dress. Then I noticed that it's lower cut than what I like.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, can you begin by introducing me to each of your family members, starting on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting on the left that's my granddaughter, one of my two granddaughters, Susan Haas. This is my son, Henry. This is Katie's mother, Eve Fraser. And that's Katie, Henry's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Haas, is there anything that you would like to say about your mother, or your family's experiences in Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: HENRY (SON): Yes. The older I get, the more I realize the significance of my parents' experience. And the more I realize the amount of courage and determination that it took in order to make the decision to first of all, leave Germany. Secondly, to go to a strange foreign land with a completely different language and culture; and then the determination it took to overcome the war years, considering the health issues and the safety issues and the whatever; and I think-- I think to have survived all of that, to have come out of it, and then to come to the United States, and to still have the vigor and the determination to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --take advantage of the benefits of being an American citizen and frankly, along the way also, instilling in me the determination to take advantage of the opportunities that are available in this country, I think the magnitude of all of that just grows on me the-- the older I get. And having been there as a child in China, and having a child's recollection, but yet, sufficient to know what it was like, I-- I think-- I think makes that all just more meaningful. And so if I to ever-- ever publicly express my-- my thoughts and my thanks, I-- I do so now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything anybody else would like to say? Susan, would you like to say anything to your grandmother about knowing of her experiences? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: SUSAN (GRANDDAUGHTER): I just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I think it's incredible that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --my family actually went through-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the things that they went through. And-- [SIGHS] For my grandma, Nani, to be able to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --live every day as-- as a normal person, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- and being the beautiful, wonderful lady that you are, is just absolutely amazing to me. [CRYING] And you know, even though I was first generation American and grew up here in America, and had a very different experience than my dad and my grandma and my grandfather, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it has also been a very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --real part of my life and has made me become the person that I am today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] --I would also have to agree that the older that I-- the older I get, the-- the more I realize just how important-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --what happened not important-- I just realize how much-- how much it means to me more and more, the older that I get. And this interview is very important to me. Because it's important for me to know what happened in order to tell my children what happened, and to let everybody know what happened, so something like that doesn't ever happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Katie?

speaker: interviewee
text: KATIE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I had the-- the interesting experiences through my mother, living in England during the war, and during World War II. And of course, we saw the war from a different perspective. My mother, obviously, is a-- a woman in the eyes of a little girl born in the middle of an air raid. But we-- we knew what was happening to us. And we could fight back. And it-- it struck me when I wrote Gerda's story for her recently, documenting her escape from Germany and her arrival in China, and what happened till they came to the United States. As I-- as I rewrote it, as she went from Germany to Czechoslovakia to Hungary to Italy, back to Germany, just back and forth, trying to find a way out. And then getting smuggled into France. I mean, she obviously covered, probably, all that with you. As I sat at my computer and-- and wrote it and rewrote it, I went through a whole variety of emotions. Because I-- hindsight, at this point, is kind of interesting. I don't know if Gerda realized every day, and also Henry's father, John, and-- and his father, Samuel, that they were flirting with death every single day. They absolutely were this far away from being picked up and being killed. And it's-- it's just an amazing story. And then-- then to top it off-- [LAUGHS] --to land in a country, China, you know, they don't-- they speak German. They don't speak Chinese. It's a completely different culture. To survive that and then come to another country with a completely different language, a different culture, I mean, that could ruin a lot of people. And you have to admire Gerda's fortitude and-- and psychological strength. And the psychological strength of everybody who went through that, to able to pull themselves together and say, by golly, despite everything that's happened, we're going to make a go of this. And-- and they did, wonderfully, just wonderfully. So for future generations-- [LAUGHS] There's no complaints. [LAUGHS] You take a look at this story, you just can't start complaining. That's all. You just take a look at your life and get on with it. Because we're very fortunate in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eve, is there anything that you would like to say, being a part of this family? EVE (EXTENDED FAMILY): Well, you would like me to say something? KATIE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Mhm. Is there anything you would like to say, knowing Gerda's story from Germany, versus you coming from England? EVE (EXTENDED FAMILY): I came from Scotland. From Scotland. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You experienced--

speaker: interviewer
text: KATIE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): What would you-- what would you like to say, mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: EVE (EXTENDED FAMILY): I-- I left Scotland. I left England because we moved to England. Because my husband was in the Royal Navy. And we had to move to there because that's where he-- he was given a position.

speaker: interviewer
text: KATIE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): But what would you like to say about Gerda's story? And Gerda's experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: EVE (EXTENDED FAMILY): I've never-- I only found about Gerda's experience when I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: KATIE (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Right. OK. Thank you. And Mrs. Haas, is there anything that you would like say to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Like I always tell everybody, we came out all right. So I feel I'm lucky. And the way we went through that, we didn't-- like Katie said, we didn't really realize how close we are, or came to death. It didn't occur to us. We-- when you're young and you're in your 20's, and also when we came to America, we were in our 30's. You still have that drive. And I think, always, how lucky we were compared to all the other people, especially they were in the camps. So the Shanghai is OK. It-- it wasn't good. We didn't-- we were hungry, but-- and had no clothes. But it still-- was still lucky. You always find the good in all situations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, tape stop.
